lessons.title: SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY
lessons.concept: Every query answers: which columns (SELECT), from which table (FROM), which rows (WHERE), in what order (ORDER BY). SQL executes FROM → WHERE → SELECT → ORDER BY — knowing this order explains most beginner errors, like why you can't use a SELECT alias inside WHERE.
lessons.example: Used-car marketplace: find recent premium leads.
lessons.code:
SELECT lead_id, city, budget
FROM leads
WHERE budget > 800000
  AND created_at >= '2026-07-01'
ORDER BY budget DESC
LIMIT 20;
lessons.tryit: Write a query for: the 10 cheapest Maruti cars listed this month, showing car_id, model, price.

quiz.q: Why does WHERE price_bucket = 'High' fail when price_bucket is an alias created in SELECT?
opts: WHERE runs before SELECT, so the alias doesn't exist yet
Strings can't be compared in WHERE
quiz.why: Logical execution order is FROM → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → ORDER BY. The alias is born in SELECT — after WHERE has already run.
quiz.approach: Repeat the expression in WHERE, or wrap the query in a subquery/CTE and filter outside.

quiz.q: ORDER BY with no direction specified sorts…
quiz.why: ASC is the default in every major database.
quiz.approach: Always write ASC/DESC explicitly in shared code — intent beats defaults.

lessons.title: GROUP BY, aggregates, HAVING
lessons.concept: GROUP BY collapses rows sharing a value into one row per group; aggregates (COUNT, SUM, AVG, MIN, MAX) summarise each group. WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after. Rule: every non-aggregated column in SELECT must appear in GROUP BY.
lessons.example: Leads per city, keeping only serious markets.
lessons.code:
SELECT city, COUNT(*) AS leads,
       AVG(budget) AS avg_budget
FROM leads
GROUP BY city
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 100
ORDER BY leads DESC;
lessons.tryit: Get average selling price and cars sold per fuel_type in July, only for fuel types with 50+ sales.

quiz.q: COUNT(*) vs COUNT(phone) — the difference?
opts: COUNT(*) counts all rows; COUNT(phone) skips NULL phones
quiz.why: COUNT(column) counts non-NULL values only — a common source of silently wrong metrics.
quiz.approach: Use COUNT(*) for row counts; COUNT(col) deliberately when you mean 'rows where col exists'.

lessons.title: JOINs without fear
lessons.concept: INNER JOIN keeps only matching rows from both tables; LEFT JOIN keeps all left rows and fills NULLs where the right side has no match. The most damaging beginner bug: a LEFT JOIN quietly becomes an INNER JOIN when you filter the right table in WHERE instead of ON.
lessons.example: All leads with their (optional) test drives.
lessons.code:
SELECT l.lead_id, t.drive_date
FROM leads l
LEFT JOIN test_drives t
  ON t.lead_id = l.lead_id
 AND t.status = 'completed';
-- putting t.status in WHERE would drop
-- every lead with no test drive!
lessons.tryit: Count leads that never booked a test drive (hint: LEFT JOIN + WHERE t.lead_id IS NULL).

quiz.q: A LEFT JOIN B returns 1,000 rows; A has 800 rows. What happened?
opts: B has duplicate matches for some A rows
LEFT JOIN adds B's unmatched rows
quiz.why: Joins multiply: one A row matching three B rows becomes three rows. Row explosion after a join = duplicate keys on the right side.
quiz.approach: Before joining, verify grain: SELECT key, COUNT(*) FROM B GROUP BY key HAVING COUNT(*)>1.

lessons.title: CTEs & subqueries
lessons.concept: A CTE (WITH block) names an intermediate result so you build logic in readable steps — filter first, aggregate second, rank third. Anything a subquery does, a CTE does more readably; chained CTEs are the standard shape of real analyst SQL.
lessons.example: City conversion rates, step by step.
lessons.code:
WITH sold AS (
  SELECT city, COUNT(*) n_sold
  FROM orders GROUP BY city
), leads AS (
  SELECT city, COUNT(*) n_leads
  FROM leads GROUP BY city
)
SELECT l.city,
       1.0*s.n_sold/l.n_leads AS conv
FROM leads l JOIN sold s USING(city)
ORDER BY conv DESC;
lessons.tryit: Using two CTEs, compute month-wise average order value and month-wise refund rate in one result.

quiz.q: Main advantage of CTEs over nested subqueries?
quiz.why: Performance is usually identical (optimizers inline both); the win is maintainability and debuggability — you can test each step alone.
quiz.approach: Name CTEs by what they contain (monthly_sales), not step1/step2.

lessons.title: CASE & conditional aggregation
lessons.concept: CASE WHEN is SQL's if/else. Its superpower is inside aggregates: SUM(CASE WHEN … THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) counts a condition per group — letting one query produce a whole pivot-style summary instead of five separate queries.
lessons.example: One-query funnel by city.
lessons.code:
SELECT city,
  COUNT(*) leads,
  SUM(CASE WHEN stage='test_drive' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS drives,
  SUM(CASE WHEN stage='sold' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS sold
FROM leads
GROUP BY city;
lessons.tryit: Build a query returning, per month: total orders, share of orders paid by 'loan', share above ₹5L.

quiz.q: AVG(CASE WHEN sold=1 THEN 1.0 ELSE 0 END) computes…
quiz.why: The average of a 1/0 flag is exactly the proportion of 1s — the cleanest rate idiom in SQL.
quiz.approach: Use 1.0 (not 1) to force decimal division in engines that do integer math.

lessons.title: Dates, cohorts & retention shape
lessons.concept: Real analysis lives on time: DATE_TRUNC('month', ts) buckets timestamps; date arithmetic builds cohort ages. A cohort query groups users by first-activity month, then measures behaviour N months later — the backbone of retention and repeat-purchase analysis.
lessons.example: Month-1 repeat rate by signup cohort.
lessons.code:
WITH firsts AS (
  SELECT user_id,
         DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(order_at)) cohort
  FROM orders GROUP BY user_id
)
SELECT f.cohort, COUNT(DISTINCT o.user_id) m1_buyers
FROM firsts f
JOIN orders o ON o.user_id=f.user_id
 AND DATE_TRUNC('month', o.order_at) = f.cohort + INTERVAL '1 month'
GROUP BY f.cohort;
lessons.tryit: Compute weekly active leads (distinct lead_id per ISO week) for the last 8 weeks.

quiz.q: Why prefer DATE_TRUNC over GROUP BY EXTRACT(MONTH …)?
opts: EXTRACT merges the same month across different years
quiz.why: EXTRACT(MONTH) returns 1–12, so July 2025 and July 2026 collapse into one bucket. DATE_TRUNC keeps year+month distinct.
quiz.approach: Bucket with DATE_TRUNC; use EXTRACT only for seasonality questions on purpose.

lessons.title: Window functions
lessons.concept: Window functions compute across related rows without collapsing them: ROW_NUMBER ranks within a partition, LAG looks at the previous row, SUM() OVER builds running totals. Anywhere you think 'per group, but keep the rows', you want a window, not a GROUP BY.
lessons.example: Latest status row per lead — the classic dedupe.
lessons.tryit: Using LAG, compute month-over-month % change in city-level sales.

quiz.q: ROW_NUMBER vs RANK on ties?
opts: RANK gives ties the same number and skips ahead; ROW_NUMBER never ties
quiz.why: Scores 90,90,80 → RANK 1,1,3; ROW_NUMBER 1,2,3 (tie broken arbitrarily unless ORDER BY disambiguates).
quiz.approach: Deduping → ROW_NUMBER with a deterministic ORDER BY. Leaderboards → RANK/DENSE_RANK.

quiz.q: Which needs a window function?
opts: Each order alongside its city's average order value
quiz.why: 'Each row + its group's aggregate' = AVG(...) OVER (PARTITION BY city). GROUP BY would collapse the orders.
quiz.approach: Keep rows + add group context → window. Collapse to one row per group → GROUP BY.

lessons.title: Why queries are slow
lessons.concept: Most slow analyst queries do one of: scan far more rows than needed (missing partition/date filter), explode rows via a bad join, or sort huge sets (ORDER BY/DISTINCT on millions of rows). EXPLAIN shows the plan; the fix is usually filter earlier, join on keys, aggregate before joining.
lessons.example: On Snowflake/BigQuery-style warehouses, filtering by the partition/cluster column (usually date) is the single biggest cost lever — SELECT * over a year costs ~365× a day's scan.
lessons.code:
-- bad: join then filter
SELECT ... FROM big_events e
JOIN users u ON u.id=e.user_id
WHERE e.event_date = CURRENT_DATE;
-- better: filter/aggregate first
WITH today AS (
  SELECT * FROM big_events
  WHERE event_date = CURRENT_DATE)
SELECT ... FROM today t
JOIN users u ON u.id=t.user_id;
lessons.tryit: Take your slowest saved query, run EXPLAIN, and identify: scanned rows, join order, and any sort step.

quiz.q: A dashboard query got 10× slower after adding one JOIN. First thing to check?
opts: Join key duplicates causing row explosion
quiz.why: Fan-out is the usual culprit: the joined table has many rows per key, multiplying the working set.
quiz.approach: Check the joined table's grain; pre-aggregate it to one row per key in a CTE before joining.

lessons.title: Star schemas & modeling
lessons.concept: Warehouses model data as fact tables (events/transactions: one row per order) surrounded by dimension tables (descriptive: customer, car, city, date). Facts are long and numeric; dims are wide and textual. BI tools are built for this shape — it's why your Power BI models want it too.
lessons.example: orders_fact (order_id, car_id, customer_id, date_id, amount) joined to dim_car (car_id, brand, model, fuel), dim_customer, dim_date. A 'sales by brand by quarter' query touches one fact + two small dims.
lessons.code:
SELECT d.quarter, c.brand,
       SUM(f.amount) sales
FROM orders_fact f
JOIN dim_car c USING(car_id)
JOIN dim_date d USING(date_id)
GROUP BY 1,2;
lessons.tryit: Sketch fact/dim tables for FASTag recharges: what's the fact grain, and which 4 dimensions matter?

quiz.q: 'One row per order line' is the fact table's…
quiz.why: Grain = what exactly one row represents. Every modeling decision and every metric definition hangs off it.
quiz.approach: State the grain in the table's name or docs; refuse to add columns that break it.

lessons.title: Variables, lists, dicts
lessons.concept: Python's two workhorse containers: lists (ordered, index by position) and dicts (key→value lookups). Analysts live in dicts-of-lists and lists-of-dicts — every JSON API response is exactly that.
lessons.example: A lead as a dict; a day's leads as a list of dicts.
lessons.code:
lead = {"city": "Gurugram",
        "budget": 900000}
leads = [lead, {"city": "Noida",
                "budget": 500000}]
big = [l for l in leads
       if l["budget"] > 600000]
print(len(big))  # 1
lessons.tryit: Given a list of price ints, build a new list with 18% GST added to each (list comprehension).

quiz.q: leads[0]["city"] returns…
quiz.why: Index the list first (row), then the dict key (field) — same mental model as table[row][column].
quiz.approach: Use .get("city") when a key might be missing to avoid KeyError.

lessons.title: Loops & functions
lessons.concept: Functions turn repeated analysis steps into named, testable units — the difference between a script you fear and one you reuse. Default arguments, early returns, and f-strings cover 90% of daily needs.
lessons.example: One function to bucket any budget.
lessons.tryit: Write monthly_growth(vals) returning a list of % changes between consecutive values.

quiz.q: What does a function return with no return statement?
quiz.why: Python functions implicitly return None — the classic cause of mysterious 'None' in output.
quiz.approach: Always return explicitly in analysis helpers; None propagating into math throws later, not here.

lessons.title: pandas: filter, group, join
lessons.concept: pandas is SQL in Python: df[mask] ≈ WHERE, groupby().agg() ≈ GROUP BY, merge() ≈ JOIN. The mental leap is vectorization — operate on whole columns at once, never loop over rows.
lessons.example: City summary in four lines.
lessons.code:
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("leads.csv")
hot = df[df.budget > 8e5]
out = (hot.groupby("city")
      .agg(leads=("lead_id","count"),
           avg_budget=("budget","mean")))
print(out.sort_values("leads"))
lessons.tryit: Load two CSVs (orders, refunds) and compute refund rate per month using merge + groupby.

quiz.q: Idiomatic pandas for 'rows where city is Delhi AND budget>5L'?
quiz.why: Boolean masks combine with & | (bitwise), each condition parenthesised; Python's 'and' fails on Series.
quiz.approach: Complex filters → build named masks first: is_delhi = df.city.eq('Delhi'); df[is_delhi & is_big].

lessons.title: Cleaning messy data
lessons.concept: Real files arrive broken: mixed types, stray spaces, duplicate rows, NULLs. The standard pass: fix dtypes (to_datetime, to_numeric), strip/normalise strings, drop_duplicates with an explicit subset, then decide NULL policy per column — never a blanket fillna(0).
lessons.example: A crash-course cleanup.
lessons.code:
df["price"] = pd.to_numeric(
  df.price.astype(str).str.replace(",",""),
  errors="coerce")
df["city"] = df.city.str.strip().str.title()
df = df.drop_duplicates(subset=["lead_id"])
missing = df.price.isna().mean()
print(f"{missing:.1%} prices missing")
lessons.tryit: Given a phone column with '+91', spaces and dashes mixed in, produce clean 10-digit strings and count invalid ones.

quiz.q: Why is fillna(0) on price usually wrong?
opts: 0 becomes a real price and poisons averages/minimums
quiz.why: A missing price isn't a free car. AVG, MIN, and 'cheapest listing' all silently corrupt.
quiz.approach: Keep NaN and use skipna behaviour, or impute deliberately (median by segment) and flag imputed rows.

lessons.title: EDA & quick charts
lessons.concept: Exploratory Data Analysis is a checklist, not art: shape and dtypes → describe() → missingness → distributions (hist) → relationships (groupby, scatter) → time trends. Ten minutes of EDA prevents ten days of wrong dashboards.
lessons.example: The 6-line profile.
lessons.code:
print(df.shape, df.dtypes, sep="\n")
print(df.describe(include="all"))
print(df.isna().mean().sort_values())
df.price.hist(bins=40)
df.groupby(df.created.dt.month).size().plot()
lessons.tryit: Profile any CSV you use at work: rows, % missing per column, top 5 categories of each text column, price histogram.

quiz.q: Mean price ₹6.2L, median ₹4.1L. The distribution is…
quiz.why: Mean dragged above median = long right tail (a few very expensive cars).
quiz.approach: Report median + percentiles for money columns; investigate the tail separately.

lessons.title: Automation & APIs
lessons.concept: The analyst force-multiplier: a script that pulls data (requests.get on an API), transforms it (pandas), and writes output (CSV/Sheet/DB) — scheduled to run without you. Add retries, timeouts, and logging; a silent broken cron is worse than none.
lessons.example: Skeleton of every data-pull script.
lessons.code:
import requests, pandas as pd
r = requests.get(url, timeout=30)
r.raise_for_status()
df = pd.DataFrame(r.json()["rows"])
df.to_csv("daily_leads.csv", index=False)
print(f"wrote {len(df)} rows")
lessons.tryit: Write a script that reads this app's data.json from your GitHub Pages URL and prints today's headline count per tag.

quiz.q: Why call r.raise_for_status()?
opts: Turns HTTP errors (403/500) into visible exceptions instead of silently parsing an error page
quiz.why: Without it, a failed API call returns error HTML/JSON that your parser mangles into garbage data downstream.
quiz.approach: Fail loudly at the boundary; log status + first 200 chars of body on failure.

lessons.title: Import, model, relate
lessons.concept: Power BI's flow: Get Data → clean in Power Query (M) → model relationships → write measures (DAX) → visualise. The model view is where dashboards are won: single-direction, one-to-many relationships from dimension tables into fact tables.
lessons.example: Load orders (fact) + car master and date table (dims); relate dim_car[car_id] 1→* orders[car_id]. Now one 'brand' slicer filters everything.
lessons.tryit: Rebuild one Excel report you maintain as: one fact query + two dim queries, related in model view — no merged mega-table.

quiz.q: Where should row-level cleanup (split columns, fix types, remove rows) happen?
quiz.why: Power Query shapes data once at refresh; DAX recalculates at every interaction. Cleanup in DAX = slow visuals.
quiz.approach: Rule: shape in Power Query (or upstream SQL), calculate business logic in DAX.

lessons.title: Visuals that don't lie
lessons.concept: Chart choice is grammar: trends → line, comparison → bar, composition at a point → stacked/100%, distribution → histogram, relationship → scatter. Slicers filter pages; drill-through jumps to a detail page carrying context. Avoid pies beyond 3 slices and dual axes without labels.
lessons.example: A sales page: KPI cards (sales, orders, AOV) on top, monthly trend line, city bar chart, and a drill-through to order-level detail per city.
lessons.tryit: Take one crowded report page you own and cut it to: 3 KPI cards, 2 charts, 1 slicer. Note what you removed and why.

quiz.q: Best visual for 'sales trend across 18 months for 3 cities'?
opts: Line chart, one line per city
quiz.why: Lines show trend and comparison simultaneously; 18 bar clusters are unreadable; pies can't show time.
quiz.approach: If lines cross confusingly, use small multiples (one mini-line per city).

lessons.title: DAX: CALCULATE & filter context
lessons.concept: Every DAX measure evaluates inside a filter context (the slicers/rows/columns acting on it). CALCULATE is the only function that changes that context — add filters, remove them (ALL), or keep some (ALLEXCEPT). Understand this and DAX stops being magic.
lessons.example: Share of city in total sales.
lessons.code:
Total Sales = SUM(orders[amount])
All City Sales = CALCULATE(
  [Total Sales], ALL(dim_city))
City Share = DIVIDE(
  [Total Sales], [All City Sales])
lessons.tryit: Write a measure 'Premium Sales' = sales where price > 8L, that still respects the user's city and month slicers.

quiz.q: ALL(dim_city) inside CALCULATE does what?
opts: Removes any city filter for that measure, so it computes the grand total
quiz.why: ALL strips the city filter from the context — the standard denominator trick for shares and % of total.
quiz.approach: % of total pattern: DIVIDE(measure, CALCULATE(measure, ALL(dim))).

lessons.title: Time intelligence
lessons.concept: MTD/YTD/vs-last-month all require a proper date dimension: a continuous date table marked as 'date table', related to the fact. Then TOTALYTD, DATEADD, SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR work. Without it, time intelligence silently misbehaves on gaps.
lessons.example: Month-over-month growth.
lessons.code:
Sales LM = CALCULATE([Total Sales],
  DATEADD(dim_date[date], -1, MONTH))
MoM % = DIVIDE(
  [Total Sales] - [Sales LM],
  [Sales LM])
lessons.tryit: Add YTD sales and MoM% to your main report; validate MoM against two months you can hand-check.

quiz.q: Why does your calendar table need every date, even no-sale days?
opts: DATEADD/YTD need continuous dates to shift periods correctly
quiz.why: Time functions navigate the date table itself; gaps make 'same period last year' land on missing rows and return blanks.
quiz.approach: Generate with CALENDAR(MINDATE, MAXDATE) or a dates table in SQL; mark as date table.

lessons.title: Star schema in the model
lessons.concept: Import mode + star schema is the performance default: narrow fact, small dims, single-direction filters. Avoid bi-directional relationships (ambiguity, slowness) and snowflaking dims into chains. One date dim can play many roles (order date, delivery date) via inactive relationships + USERELATIONSHIP.
lessons.example: Orders fact with order_date and delivery_date both relating to dim_date — one active, one inactive; a 'Delivered Sales' measure activates the second with USERELATIONSHIP.
lessons.code:
Delivered Sales = CALCULATE(
 [Total Sales],
 USERELATIONSHIP(
  orders[delivery_date],
  dim_date[date]))
lessons.tryit: Audit your model view: list every bi-directional arrow and justify or fix each one.

quiz.q: Main risk of bi-directional relationships everywhere?
opts: Ambiguous filter paths and wrong totals under some slicer combos
quiz.why: Multiple filter paths between tables can contradict; the engine picks one and your totals quietly change meaning.
quiz.approach: Default single direction; use bi-directional only for many-to-many bridges you've reasoned through.

lessons.title: Performance & refresh
lessons.concept: Slow report? Diagnose in order: too many visuals per page → heavy DAX (iterators over big tables) → wide imported columns you never use → DirectQuery where import would do. Performance Analyzer shows per-visual timings. Incremental refresh keeps big fact tables fresh without full reloads.
lessons.example: A 3M-row orders table refreshed daily: incremental refresh loads only the last 3 days' partition; the report drops from 40-min to 2-min refresh.
lessons.tryit: Run Performance Analyzer on your slowest page; record the top 2 visuals by DAX time and one fix each.

quiz.q: First lever when a single page takes 12s to render?
opts: Reduce visual count / simplify the heaviest DAX visual (check Performance Analyzer)
quiz.why: Each visual fires queries; pages with 20+ visuals or an iterator-heavy matrix dominate render time.
quiz.approach: Measure first (Performance Analyzer), then fix the top offender — don't guess.

lessons.title: Dimensions, measures, marks
lessons.concept: Tableau splits fields into dimensions (slice: city, brand) and measures (aggregate: sales, count). Drag to Rows/Columns to define the grid; the Marks card (colour, size, label, detail) encodes more fields. Blue pills = discrete headers, green = continuous axes — that colour explains most 'why does it look like this' confusion.
lessons.example: Sales by city bar chart: city → Rows, SUM(sales) → Columns, fuel_type → Colour. Three drags, done.
lessons.tryit: Recreate one Excel chart in Tableau in under 5 minutes: one dimension, one measure, one colour encoding.

quiz.q: A green pill on Columns creates…
quiz.why: Green = continuous → axis; blue = discrete → headers. Same field can often be either — right-click to convert.
quiz.approach: Dates especially: discrete YEAR/MONTH parts vs continuous truncated dates give different charts on purpose.

lessons.title: LOD expressions
lessons.concept: Level-of-Detail expressions compute at a grain you choose, ignoring the view's grain: {FIXED [customer]: MIN([order_date])} gives each customer's first order date on every row. FIXED ignores view filters (mostly), INCLUDE/EXCLUDE adjust relative to the view — FIXED covers 90% of real uses.
lessons.example: New vs repeat revenue: flag = [order_date] = {FIXED [customer_id]: MIN([order_date])} → colour by flag.
lessons.code:
{FIXED [customer_id] :
  MIN([order_date])}
lessons.tryit: Build 'share of city in national sales' using {FIXED : SUM(sales)} as denominator.

quiz.q: Why does a plain SUM(sales)/SUM(sales) always show 100%?
opts: Both sums evaluate at the same view grain, so they're equal in every cell
quiz.why: Numerator and denominator share the view's context. The denominator must be pinned to a coarser grain — exactly what a FIXED LOD does.
quiz.approach: Percent-of-total = measure / {FIXED : SUM(measure)} (or table calcs when layout-relative is fine).

lessons.title: Dashboards & actions
lessons.concept: A dashboard is sheets + interactivity: filter actions (click a city bar → all sheets filter to it), highlight actions, and parameter controls (user-chosen metric or threshold). Design mobile-fit containers, 3–5 sheets max, filters top-left where eyes start.
lessons.example: Exec view: KPI row, trend line, city map; clicking a city filters the trend — one filter action, no visible filter dropdowns.
lessons.tryit: Add one filter action to an existing dashboard replacing two dropdown filters; time how much faster a question gets answered.

quiz.q: Parameter vs filter — key difference?
opts: A parameter is a single user-chosen value usable inside calculations; a filter subsets data directly
quiz.why: Parameters feed formulas (switch metric, set threshold); filters cut rows. Combined, they make one sheet act like five.
quiz.approach: Metric-switcher pattern: parameter [Choose Metric] + CASE calculation using it.

lessons.title: Performance & calc hygiene
lessons.concept: Slow workbooks: too many quick filters (each queries the source), high-cardinality dimensions on detail, row-level calcs that belong upstream, and live connections doing warehouse work per click. Extracts + aggregating before Tableau + fewer filters fix most of it.
lessons.example: A 12-filter dashboard on a live 50M-row connection → move to an extract aggregated to day×city×brand, 4 filters + 1 action: loads in 2s instead of 45s.
lessons.tryit: Open your slowest workbook's Performance Recording; identify the single longest query and whether an extract or upstream aggregate would kill it.

quiz.q: Cheapest big win for a slow live-connection dashboard?
quiz.why: Extracts move query cost to refresh time and Tableau's engine is optimized for them; users stop hammering the warehouse per click.
quiz.approach: Extract at the coarsest grain the dashboard truly needs — not raw rows.

lessons.title: Agile vs waterfall, and Scrum's cast
lessons.concept: Waterfall plans everything upfront; agile ships small increments and corrects course from feedback. Scrum: a Product Owner (orders the backlog by value), a Scrum Master (removes blockers, guards process), and the Development Team. Work happens in fixed-length sprints (usually 2 weeks) producing something usable.
lessons.example: Your dashboard request flow is agile in miniature: stakeholders ask, you ship a v1 in days, feedback reshapes v2 — instead of a 3-month 'perfect' spec.
lessons.tryit: Map your current project: who acts as PO (prioritises), who unblocks, what's the 'increment' you ship each week?

quiz.q: Who decides the order of the product backlog?
quiz.why: The PO owns value-ordering; the team owns how much fits a sprint and how it's built.
quiz.approach: As an analyst, feed the PO data on impact — that's how your items climb the backlog.

lessons.title: Ceremonies & artifacts
lessons.concept: Four ceremonies: sprint planning (choose sprint backlog), daily stand-up (15 min: progress/plan/blockers), review (demo to stakeholders), retrospective (improve the process). Artifacts: product backlog, sprint backlog, increment. Everything else is optional decoration.
lessons.example: Good stand-up line: 'Funnel dashboard: city drill-through done; today DAX for MoM; blocked on prod schema access — need Vikas.' Twenty seconds, complete.
lessons.tryit: Write tomorrow's stand-up update in exactly three lines: yesterday / today / blockers.

quiz.q: The retrospective is about…
opts: How the team works — process improvements for next sprint
quiz.why: Review inspects the product; retro inspects the process. Teams that skip retros repeat the same friction forever.
quiz.approach: Bring one data point to retros ('we spent 30% of sprint on ad-hoc pulls') — analysts make retros concrete.

lessons.title: User stories & acceptance criteria
lessons.concept: A story frames work by outcome: 'As a <role>, I want <thing>, so that <value>.' Acceptance criteria make 'done' testable. For analysts this is gold for dashboard requests — it forces stakeholders to state the decision the dashboard serves.
lessons.example: 'As a retail head, I want daily city-level conversion with a 7-day trend, so that I can rebalance inventory weekly.' AC: refreshes by 8am; matches finance numbers ±1%; loads <5s.
lessons.tryit: Rewrite your last vague request ('need a sales report') as a story with 3 acceptance criteria.

quiz.q: Best acceptance criterion?
opts: 'Shows MTD sales by city, matches finance report ±1%, refreshed by 8:00 IST'
quiz.why: Testable, specific, has a pass/fail. The others are vibes.
quiz.approach: Each AC should be checkable by someone else without asking you.

lessons.title: Flow metrics: velocity, burndown, Kanban
lessons.concept: Velocity = story points completed per sprint (planning aid, never a performance KPI). Burndown charts show remaining work vs time. Kanban skips sprints: continuous flow with WIP limits; its metrics — cycle time and throughput — often fit analyst/support work better than Scrum.
lessons.example: A BI team with constant ad-hoc requests fits Kanban: a WIP limit of 3 exposes overload instantly; cycle time per request becomes your SLA evidence.
lessons.tryit: Track your own cycle time for one week: request arrival → delivered. The distribution will surprise you.

quiz.q: Why is comparing two teams' velocities meaningless?
opts: Points are relative estimates calibrated within each team, not a shared unit
quiz.why: A '5' in one team is a '13' in another; velocity only forecasts that same team's capacity.
quiz.approach: Use velocity for planning conversations; use cycle time/throughput when stakeholders want speed evidence.

lessons.title: 'Tell me about yourself' — the 90-second version
lessons.concept: Interviewers use this to decide what to probe next, so you control the map. Structure: current role and scale (one line) → 2 quantified wins (one line each) → why this role/company (one line). Under 90 seconds, no childhood, no hobbies, end pointing at the job.
lessons.example: 'I'm a Business Analyst at a used-car marketplace, working as the data/BI analyst for a product pod. Two things I'm proud of: consolidating event tracking across a platform migration for our payments flows, and building the daily-insights reporting our pod leadership reads each morning. I'm looking for a role where the analytics work is deeper on the modeling side — which is what drew me to this one.'
lessons.tryit: Write your version, read it aloud with a timer. Cut until it's under 90 seconds. Every claim must survive a follow-up 'tell me more'.

quiz.q: The main job of your 'tell me about yourself' answer?
opts: Set the 2-3 topics you WANT to be probed on
quiz.why: Interviewers follow the threads you hand them. A rambling answer hands them random threads; a structured one hands them your strongest.
quiz.approach: End each win with a number — numbers are the threads interviewers pull.

lessons.title: SQL rounds: the five questions that keep coming
lessons.concept: Analyst SQL interviews recycle five patterns: latest-row-per-entity (ROW_NUMBER), second-highest (DENSE_RANK or OFFSET), month-over-month change (LAG), funnel conversion (conditional aggregation), and duplicate detection (GROUP BY HAVING COUNT>1). Master these cold — typed, not just recognised.
lessons.example: 'Get each customer's most recent order' — the single most-asked analyst SQL question in India right now.
lessons.code:
SELECT * FROM (
  SELECT o.*, ROW_NUMBER() OVER(
    PARTITION BY customer_id
    ORDER BY order_at DESC) rn
  FROM orders o) t
WHERE rn=1;
lessons.tryit: Without looking anything up, write all five patterns against an imaginary orders(customer_id, order_at, amount, status) table. Check yourself against the SQL track lessons.

quiz.q: Interviewer: 'why ROW_NUMBER not GROUP BY here?'
opts: GROUP BY can't return the full row — it collapses; window functions keep every column
quiz.why: GROUP BY customer_id + MAX(order_at) loses the rest of the row; joining back is clumsy. Window functions answer 'whole row per group' directly.
quiz.approach: Say the reason BEFORE writing code — interviewers score the reasoning, not just the syntax.

lessons.title: The dashboard case round
lessons.concept: 'Design a dashboard for the city operations head' is a thinking test, not a drawing test. The winning order: 1) clarify the DECISION the user makes weekly, 2) derive 3-5 metrics from that decision, 3) state grain and refresh, 4) sketch layout (KPIs top, trend middle, drill bottom), 5) name one metric you'd refuse to add and why.
lessons.example: For a used-car city head: decision = where to rebalance inventory and staffing. Metrics: leads→sale conversion by city, inventory aging buckets, days-to-sale trend, refund/RTO rate as guardrail. Refresh daily 8am; grain city × day.
lessons.tryit: Pick any manager you know. Write their weekly decision in one sentence, then the 4 metrics that serve it. If a metric doesn't change the decision, cut it.

quiz.q: Best FIRST sentence in a dashboard case?
opts: 'What decision will the user make with this, and how often?'
'I'd add filters for city and date'
quiz.why: Tools and filters are downstream. Anchoring on the decision shows senior thinking and makes every later choice defensible.
quiz.approach: Literally memorise the question — it's the highest-value sentence in any BI interview.

lessons.title: Product sense & guesstimates
lessons.concept: Marketplace analyst interviews test metric instinct: know a north star (e.g. successful transactions), the funnel to it (visits→leads→inspections→listings→sales), and guardrails (refunds, NPS, time-to-sale). Guesstimates ('how many used cars sell monthly in Gurugram?') score your structure: population → segment → rate → sanity-check, stated aloud.
lessons.example: Gurugram guesstimate: ~2M households → ~60% own cars (~1.2M cars) → cars change hands every ~6 years → ~200K/year → ~17K/month across all channels; organised platforms maybe 15-20% → 2.5-3.5K/month. Numbers can be off — the structure can't.
lessons.tryit: Do 'how many test drives happen daily in your city?' out loud, writing each assumption. Then vary one assumption ±50% and see if the conclusion changes.

quiz.q: What do interviewers actually grade in a guesstimate?
opts: Explicit assumptions, clean structure, and a sanity check
quiz.why: They know you don't know the number. They're watching whether your decomposition is logical and whether you check your own answer.
quiz.approach: Always end with a sanity check: 'that implies X per day, which feels right because…'

lessons.title: Behavioral rounds: STAR without the fluff
lessons.concept: Prepare 4 stories: a win with numbers, a failure you fixed, a conflict you resolved, a deadline you rescued. Tell each as Situation (1 line), Task (1 line), Action (3-4 lines, 'I' not 'we'), Result (number + lesson). If you use AI tools at work, own it as leverage — 'AI drafts, I verify and own the output' — never hide it, never overstate your unaided level.
lessons.example: Failure story shape: 'A metric I shipped double-counted refunds (S). I owned the correction (T). I traced it to a join fan-out, fixed the grain, added a reconciliation check against finance (A). Numbers matched within 0.5% and the check now runs weekly (R).'
lessons.tryit: Write all four stories in exactly 8 lines each. Practice the failure one most — it's where candidates either build trust or lose it.

quiz.q: The most common STAR mistake?
opts: Spending 80% on Situation and saying 'we' throughout the Action
quiz.why: Interviewers need YOUR actions. Long context + team credit leaves them unable to score you.
quiz.approach: One line of context, then 'I did X, I did Y' — and a numeric result.

quiz.q: Asked about a skill you're intermediate at (say DAX), best answer?
opts: 'Intermediate: I do X and Y solo, use references/AI for Z, and here's a recent example'
quiz.why: Verification exists — inflated claims collapse in the technical round. Calibrated honesty with a concrete example reads as senior.
quiz.approach: Honesty + evidence + trajectory ('currently deepening Z') beats bravado every time.

lessons.title: Your questions, their offer
lessons.concept: Your questions signal your level. Ask about the data stack's pain points, how metric definitions are governed, and what the last analyst who succeeded here did differently. On comp: never name a number first if avoidable — ask for the band; if forced, give a researched range anchored high-but-defensible, and negotiate total comp (fixed, variable, ESOP, review cycle), not just CTC.
lessons.example: Strong closer: 'What's the messiest data problem this team owns right now, and is fixing it part of this role?' — it signals you expect real work and have seen mess before.
lessons.tryit: Write your 3 closing questions and your comp range with one line of evidence for it (market data for your city/experience). Rehearse saying the range without flinching.

quiz.q: Recruiter asks current salary + expected in the first call. Best move?
opts: Share expectation as a range anchored to market data; deflect current comp politely if possible
quiz.why: 'As per norms' surrenders leverage; inflating current comp is verifiable and disqualifying. A researched expectation range is safe and strong.
quiz.approach: 'Based on the scope and market, I'm targeting X–Y total comp; I'm flexible for the right role' — then stop talking.
